When a composite is inserted onto the story canvas, its associated data sources are now added automatically — limited to sources the user has access to and that haven't already been added. This eliminates the prior manual step and reduces the risk of designer error during story assembly.

Chart Colors and Patterns Carry Over from Composites

Chart colors and patterns defined within a composite are now preserved in consuming stories, removing a manual reconciliation step and ensuring that composite-level design intent is carried through end-to-end.

For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2026

2025 Q4  ·  Discoverability and Script Reuse

Where-Used List for Composites

A where-used list is now available in the file repository and at composite design time, showing which stories consume a given composite along with name, description, owner, creation date, and modification metadata. Columns are sortable and the list is searchable — making composite lifecycle management tractable at enterprise scale.

Automatic Exposure of Script Object Functions

Custom script object functions can now be automatically exposed as reusable interface functions, eliminating the need to manually copy scripts across stories when sharing logic between composites and consuming stories.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q4 2025

2025 Q3  ·  Scripting Depth and Design-Time Flexibility

Script Access for Composite Properties

Composite properties can now be read and assigned in the script editor and calculation editor, with errors and reference lists supported — bringing composite property scripting to parity with how other object properties are handled in the SAC scripting environment.

Page-Size Setting Improvements

Composite design-time page dimensions are now configurable across a wide range: width from 128px to 7680px, and height from 32px to 7680px. For composites that need to fit precisely into specific layout contexts, this granularity matters.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q3 2025

2025 Q2  ·  Visual Fidelity and UX Alignment

Threshold Support Within Composite Definitions

Visual thresholds can now be defined and embedded within the composite itself, operating independently from story-level thresholds — no risk of cross-composite interference. The initial scope covers chart widgets with measure-based thresholds; table and dimension-based assigned color support is planned for subsequent releases.

Fixed vs. Dynamic Composite Size

A new fixed sizing mode joins the existing dynamic default, giving designers pixel-precise control over composite dimensions for layout-critical scenarios where consistency across different story canvases is required.

Unified Styling and Builder Panel Experience

The composite properties panel has been integrated into the Styling panel, and composite model configuration has been moved into the Builder panel — aligning composites with the interaction pattern used for charts and tables, and reducing cognitive overhead for story designers who work across widget types.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q2 2025

2025 Q1  ·  Foundational Capability Expansion

Global Calculations in Composite Widgets

Account, dimension, and cross-calculations can now be defined directly within composite widgets. Custom widgets with applicable calculation support are also covered. This closes a significant capability gap that had limited how analytically rich a self-contained composite could be.

Flow Layout Panel and Slider Widgets

The composite widget palette now includes Flow Layout Panel, Slider, and Range Slider. Flow Layout Panel enables composites that adapt gracefully to different screen sizes. Sliders enable interactive, dynamic composites — for instance, what-if simulation scenarios where users adjust inputs and see results update in real time.

Linked Analysis Support for Composite Target Widgets

Composites can now participate in linked analysis as target widgets. When a source widget drives a filter or selection, composite widgets — and the charts, tables, custom widgets, and text elements within them that share the same data source — will respond accordingly.

Option to Disable Model Configuration in Composites

A new Global Settings option lets composite owners lock down data model configuration, preventing story designers from substituting models when consistency must be enforced. It's unchecked by default, preserving existing flexibility.

 For details you can refer to:

What's New in SAP Analytics Cloud | Deep Dive with Product Experts | Q1 2025

Summary and Outlook

Stepping back across these 6 quarters of release notes, three clear themes emerge:

  • Governance and lifecycle management — Versioning, where-used lists, transport controls, and deep copy support are progressively turning composites from ad hoc reuse artifacts into properly governed, enterprise-grade content components.
  • Developer and designer experience — Optional arguments, auto-exposure of script functions, auto-incorporation of data sources, and unified panel UX are all reducing friction and aligning composites with the quality-of-life expectations of modern analytics developers.
  • Integration with the broader SAP ecosystem — Lite Viewer support, SAP Business Data Cloud intelligent application imports, and Datasphere model integration are broadening composites capabilities across the entire SAP Business Data Cloud, with full support for Datasphere models and an expanding set of intelligent applications

For analytics teams building on SAP Analytics Cloud, these innovations collectively lower the cost of building reusable content, raise the ceiling of what composites can express, and provide the governance infrastructure needed to manage composite libraries at enterprise scale.

We are continuously advancing composite capabilities through ongoing innovation. Please stay tuned for further updates !

entry.title: Two Ways to Build a Hierarchy in SAC and How to Choose the Right One
entry.content:
If you have ever built a SAC story showing regional sales or cost centre spending, you have used a hierarchy even if you did not think of it that way. Hierarchies let a table start at 'Global' and let users click down through continent, country, and city without leaving the page.

SAC supports two hierarchy types: level-based and parent-child. They look similar in a story but work differently, are set up differently, and suit different source data. Choosing the wrong one leads to blank values, missing nodes, or a hierarchy that simply does not behave. This post explains both types, how to set them up, how to use them in stories, and how to pick the right one.

1. The Two Hierarchy Types - What Makes Them Different

A level-based hierarchy has one column per level in your source table. A three-level geography hierarchy needs three columns: continent, country, city. Every branch goes exactly as deep as the number of level columns you define, the depth is fixed and uniform across the whole tree.

A parent-child hierarchy uses just two key columns: a node ID and a parent ID. Each row says "This node belongs to this parent". SAC reads those relationships and builds the tree automatically - depth can vary branch by branch. This is the native format of ERP and financial systems like SAP BW, BPC, and S/4HANA.

Key takeaway: Level-based = one column per level, uniform depth. Parent-child = two columns, any depth, ideal for ERP data.

                                  

2. Setting Up a Level-Based Hierarchy

First, check your source data has one column per level. For a three-level geography structure that means continent, country, and city columns - each row repeats the parent values for every leaf record.

In SAC Model Builder, open the Datasets tab, you will find a level hierarchy as shown below in image, then drag your level columns into order from Level 0 (most general) down to the most granular. Give it a clear name, save, and deploy. The hierarchy then appears as a drill-down option on any table or chart using that dimension. There are two ways to do this:

        

                        

Practical tip: use short descriptive names for level columns such as  'L0 — Continent', 'L1 — Country', 'L2 — City' rather than generic labels like 'Column A'. It makes the model far easier to maintain.

Key takeaway: One column per level in the source, configure in Model Builder, name and deploy. Available in every story that uses that model.

                   

3. Setting Up a Parent-Child Hierarchy

Your source needs two key columns: a node ID and a parent ID. The root node - the top of the tree, such as 'Global' - has no parent, so its parent field is empty. Every other node points to its immediate parent.

SAC identifies the root, finds all nodes whose parent ID matches it, then recurses down through each level automatically. Depth is driven entirely by the data - SAC does not need to be told how many levels to expect. In Model Builder, configure the dimension to reference the node ID and parent ID columns, then save and deploy.

The most common mistake is inconsistent IDs - a parent ID that does not exactly match any node ID in the table. This causes orphaned nodes that do not appear in the tree. Always validate your IDs before importing.

Key takeaway: Two columns - node ID and parent ID - are all SAC needs. Variable depth is handled automatically. Check ID consistency before importing.

                      

4. Using Hierarchies in Stories

Once a hierarchy is defined in the model, there are four interactions worth knowing in stories:

  • Drill down: Right-click a node and select Drill Down to expand just that member and hide everything else. Useful when a user wants to focus on a single branch.
  • Expand and collapse: Click ▶ on any node to reveal its children while keeping the parent total visible. The most natural way for users to browse — open what you need, leave the rest collapsed.
  • Hierarchy display setting: In table or chart properties, choose between indented (default, shows tree structure), flat list, or custom levels. Indented is best for most audiences.
  • Flattening: Filter by hierarchy level in the filter bar to show only one level at a time — for example, only countries. Gives a clean, focused view without manual collapsing.

Key takeaway: Expand/collapse for browsing, drill down for focus, flattening for presenting one level at a time. Match the interaction to what your audience actually needs.

                                              

            

5. Which Type Should You Use?

The decision is usually straightforward once you look at your source data:

  • Flat table, one column per level, uniform depth → use level-based
  • Data from SAP BW, BPC, S/4HANA, or any ERP system → use parent-child
  • Tree depth varies branch by branch → you must use parent-child
  • Unsure or building from scratch → start with level-based, migrate later if needed

The most common mistake is trying to force uneven-depth data into a level-based hierarchy. If some branches go three levels deep and others go six, level-based will produce empty columns and confusing results. Parent-child is the right tool for that job.

Key takeaway: Match the hierarchy type to your source data structure. When in doubt, level-based is simpler to set up and easier to troubleshoot.

Final Thoughts

A well-configured hierarchy makes a story feel natural and powerful , users explore data at the level they need without multiple separate reports. A badly configured one creates blank values and a loss of trust in the numbers.

The things to remember: know your source data structure before choosing a type, use level-based for flat uniform data and parent-child for ERP or variable-depth data, and learn all four story interactions, each serves a different purpose.

If a hierarchy is misbehaving, the usual culprits are a type mismatch with the source, inconsistent parent IDs, or level columns in the wrong order. Start there and you will find the issue quickly.

Thanks for reading. If hierarchies have given you grief on a real build, I would love to hear about it in the comments.

entry.title: Advanced Scripting in SAC: How to use Page Navigation & Popup Panels
entry.content:
There is a moment when a SAC story stops feeling like a report and starts feeling like an application. For me, that moment came when I discovered scripting.

SAC's script editor lets you attach logic to user actions - a button click, a selection change, a page load. Once you understand even the basics, you can build navigation flows, show and hide panels, and guide users through your story the way a well-designed app would. The gap between a standard SAC dashboard and a polished, app-like experience is often just a handful of simple scripts.

In this post I will walk through the two patterns I use most: page navigation buttons and popup panels for drill-through detail.

 

1. What SAC Scripting Is (and Is Not)

SAC uses its own event-driven scripting language - not JavaScript, not Python. Scripts run in response to things the user does. The three events you will use most are:

  • onClick - triggered when a user presses a button. The workhorse of SAC scripting.
  • onChange - triggered when an input control changes, such as a dropdown selection.
  • onInitialization - triggered when a page loads. Useful for setting default states.

The trap I fell into early was assuming scripting was a developer's domain. In reality, the patterns used in 90% of scripted stories are copy-paste simple once you have seen them once. You do not need to memorise syntax - you need to recognise the pattern and adapt it to your widget names.

Key takeaway: Think of SAC scripting as reusable building blocks, not custom code from scratch.

                            

                            

 

2. Page Navigation with Buttons

The default SAC tab strip works, but it is not controllable - every page is always visible and you cannot guide users through a logical flow. With scripting, you replace it with buttons you design yourself.

The script is one line. On the onClick event of a button widget:

    Application.showPage("PageName");

Replace PageName with the internal name of your page and you have a fully functional navigation button. I recently used this to build a three-page story with a persistent navigation bar - Home, Trends, and Detail. Stakeholders asked whether it had been built in a different tool. It had not.

The one rule I would emphasise: consistency. Same button position, same size, same style on every page. The scripting is the easy part - keeping the navigation visually consistent is where the real craft lies.

Key takeaway: Custom navigation gives you full control over the user journey. Show only what is relevant at each step and make the story feel deliberate.

3. Popup Panels for Drill-Through Detail

Sometimes you want to show detail without sending users to a new page and losing their context. A popup panel - a container widget hidden by default and revealed on demand - solves this elegantly.

The pattern has four steps:

  • Create a container widget and fill it with your detail content - a chart, a table, or both.
  • In the container's onInitialization event, write: Container_1.setVisible(false);
  • Add a trigger button on the main canvas with onClick script: Container_1.setVisible(true);
  • Add a close button inside the container with onClick script: Container_1.setVisible(false);

The first time I used this in a live presentation, there was a visible shift in how the audience engaged. The conversation moved from 'what does this chart show?' to 'can we look at that region more closely?' - which is exactly the conversation you want.

Key takeaway: Popup panels let users go deeper without losing their place. It is the closest thing SAC has to a modal dialog, and one of the most effective patterns for making a story feel designed.

                   

                                  

 

 

4. Three Rules for Scripted Stories

Scripting introduces a new category of risk - things that work during development but confuse users when the story goes live. Three rules I now apply to every scripted build:

  • Always add a close or back button. If you open something with a script, close it with a script. This cost me a live demo where a stakeholder opened a popup and could not close it. The panel covered the entire canvas. Never again.
  • Keep scripts short. More than three or four lines usually means the story design needs simplifying. Each script should do one thing.
  • Test on a small screen. Popup panels sized on a large monitor can break entirely on a laptop. Always check on the smallest screen your audience is likely to use.

Key takeaway: Scripting discipline matters more than scripting skill. A simple, reliable story beats a clever, fragile one every time.

               

Final Thoughts

Scripting moved SAC from 'dashboard builder' to 'experience designer' in my mind. When you control the navigation and the interactions, you are not just presenting data - you are designing how someone engages with it.

I have covered most of the key topics to help beginners and beyond. If you have suggestions or topics you’d like to see, feel free to comment. I will keep updating as I learn more.

Start small - one button, one popup. Even 20 minutes can make your story feel more polished.

What scripting patterns have you tried in SAC? Share in the comments!

Thanks for reading, happy scripting! ✨

entry.title: Why Your SAP Analytics Cloud Model Choice Can Make or Break Your Reporting Performance
entry.content:
One of the first decisions you make when connecting data to SAC will affect everything that follows: do you use a Live model or an Import model? It shapes how your story performs, how fresh your data is, and how much control you have over the user experience.

I got this wrong on my first proper build - not catastrophically, but wrong enough that I had to rethink the approach partway through. The turning point came when users asked why the dashboard felt slow, and I realised the problem was not SAC.

In this post I will explain what each model type does, when each is the right choice, and how to avoid the trade-off trap I fell into.

                   

1. What Each Model Type Actually Does

A Live model connects SAC directly to the source system - SAP BW, HANA, or S/4HANA - and queries it in real time. Every filter you apply, every drill you execute, sends a query back to the source. The data you see is always current, because it has never left.

An Import model works differently. SAC pulls the data into its own in-memory storage on a schedule you define. The story runs against that snapshot. Data is current as of the last successful refresh — no earlier, no later.

The key difference is not just freshness , it is where computation happens. Live models push the heavy lifting to the source system. Import models handle it inside SAC's in-memory engine. This single distinction drives almost every trade-off between the two.

Key takeaway:  Live = always fresh, source does the work. Import = snapshot in time, SAC does the work.

                     

2. When to Use a Live Model

Live models are the right choice when data freshness is genuinely critical - financial closing dashboards, operational monitoring, real-time KPI tracking. If your stakeholders need to see data updating throughout the day without waiting for a refresh, Live is usually the answer. Use a Live model when:

  • Data freshness is measured in minutes, not hours
  • The source is SAP BW or HANA with well-optimised queries
  • Data volume is too large to import efficiently
  • Governance requires that data never leaves the source system

The thing I underestimated: the performance of a Live model depends entirely on the source system. If the HANA query behind your story is well-optimised, the dashboard feels instant. If it is not, every filter and drill feels slow - and there is nothing you can fix inside SAC. That is the lesson I learned the hard way on my first build.

Key takeaway:  Live models borrow their performance from the source. Know the health of your source before you commit.

                         

3. When to Use an Import Model

For the majority of analytical use cases - trend analysis, management reporting, self-service exploration; Import models deliver a noticeably better experience. Because the data sits in SAC's in-memory engine, filtering and drilling happen in milliseconds. Consider an Import model when:

  • Fast, interactive filtering and drilling is essential
  • Data volume is manageable - millions, not billions of rows
  • Your source is non-SAP (CSV, Excel, external APIs)
  • You need to blend data from multiple sources into one model
  • Your audience does self-service exploratory analysis

The trade-off is freshness. If your data updates hourly but your refresh schedule runs daily, the story will be a day behind. For most management reporting and trend analysis, this is entirely acceptable. For operational dashboards where someone is making a decision right now, it may not be.

Key takeaway:  Import models give you speed and flexibility  but you own the refresh schedule and its consequences.

                       

4. The Decision Framework (and the Hybrid Option)

After a few builds, I settled on a simple set of questions that almost always point to the right answer:

  • Is data freshness critical (minutes)? → Live
  • Is the source SAP BW or HANA with optimised queries? → Live viable
  • Do you need to blend sources in a single model? → Import
  • Is your audience doing self-service exploratory analysis? → Import
  • Unsure? → Start with Import, migrate to Live if freshness becomes an issue

The option that surprises people most is the hybrid approach. You can use a Live model for the headline KPI tiles that must always show current data, alongside an Import model for the trend charts where interaction speed matters more than real-time precision. Both can sit in the same SAC story, and your audience will rarely notice the difference — they will just notice that everything works.

Key takeaway:  The best choice matches your data's nature and your audience's needs — not the one that sounds most impressive.

Final Thoughts

The Live vs Import question is not a technical detail you can defer. It shapes every interaction your audience will have with your story such as how fast it feels, how fresh the data is, and how much flexibility you have to evolve it later.

My rule of thumb: start every new story by asking two questions. How often does my audience actually need this data to refresh? And where does my source system's query performance really live? The answers almost always point to the right model and sometimes they point to both.

What has your experience been with Live vs Import? I would love to hear what pushed you one way or the other, drop a comment below.

Thanks for reading - happy modelling!

entry.title: Microsoft Teams as a Delivery Channel for My Metrics Reports in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP Analytics Cloud has continuously evolved its alerting and monitoring capabilities for the My Metrics List feature. Users can already receive scheduled report notifications through two established channels: the SAC Notification Center and email inbox. These channels ensure that business users stay informed about metric movements on a schedule that fits their workflow.

With the 2026 Q2 release, SAP Analytics Cloud extends this capability further by introducing Microsoft Teams as a supported delivery channel. Business users can now receive their My Metrics report notifications directly within Teams — the collaboration hub where they already spend a significant portion of their working day. This eliminates the need to switch between applications to access critical metric updates and act on them quickly.

Feature Capabilities

The Microsoft Teams delivery channel is designed to be fully consistent with the existing notification scope available today via email reports and the SAC Notification Center. The following capabilities are included:

  • Channel Configuration:  Users can select Microsoft Teams as a notification option when configuring their Metrics Report subscription. The option is available within the Distribution List step of the subscription wizard.
  • Teams Channel Email Integration:  The integration leverages Teams channel email addresses (e.g. <channelname>@<tenant>.teams.ms). Users enter the channel email into the 'Send Emails to Microsoft Teams Channel' dialog — no additional connector or app installation is required.  For sending an email to channel in Microsoft Teams please refer to here
  • Notification Content:  Each Teams notification includes all standard report details: Metrics List Name, Subscription Name, Recurrence Pattern, Start Date, End Date, an Overview of Metrics table with Latest Value, Changes in Value, Previous Value, and Latest Variance — plus clickable metric name links and a direct 'Open My Metrics' deep-link to SAC.
  • Scheduling and Trigger Respect:  Teams notifications honour existing scheduling configurations and trigger conditions set during subscription setup. No separate scheduling is needed for the Teams channel.
  • Reply in Thread:  Teams thread functionality is preserved — users can reply inline to a notification post, enabling quick discussion or follow-up directly in context.

Getting Started

Configuration is completed directly within the existing 'Subscribe to Metrics Report' wizard in SAP Analytics Cloud. No additional plugins, connectors, or admin setup are required beyond having the Teams channel email address available.

  1. Open the Subscription Wizard  From your My Metrics list, click the options menu (⋯) on any metric card and select 'Subscribe'. The 'Subscribe to Metrics Report' dialog opens. Open Subscription Dialog

     

     

  2. Configure the Schedule  In Step 2, set the Recurrence Pattern (e.g. Daily), the Start Date of First Occurrence, and the End Date. Use 'Check Availability' to validate the schedule.Define Occurence

     

     

  3. Add the Teams Channel as a Recipient  In Step 3 (Distribution List), click '+ Add Recipients', then select 'Send Emails to Microsoft Teams Channel'. Enter the Teams channel email address (available from Teams under Get email address for the channel) and click Save.Add email address of Team channel

     

     

  4. Save the Subscription  Once the Teams channel email is added to the Recipients list (alongside any individual user recipients), click Save. The subscription is now active and will deliver to both the Teams channel and any individual recipients.Save the subscription report

     

     

  5. Metrics Report in Microsoft TeamsMetrics Report in Teams Channel

     

     

     

Summary and Outlook

With the 2026 Q2 release, SAP Analytics Cloud adds Microsoft Teams as a delivery channel for My Metrics reports. Users can now receive scheduled notifications — including subscription details, a full metrics overview, and a direct link to SAC — directly in Teams, alongside the existing SAC Notification Center and email options. Setup requires no additional integration; users simply add a Teams channel email address in the existing subscription wizard.

 A lot more is coming for My Metrics and Metrics Report, and I'm excited about what's ahead. Stay tuned for updates in the near future. Until then, I'd love to hear your thoughts — leave a comment, share your thoughts. That conversation is what keeps us pointed in the right direction.

entry.title: Compass: Predictive Range Proposal "Smart Boundaries"
entry.content:
Introduction

For SAP Analytics Cloud, version 2026.12, we have integrated the capability of Smart Predict into compass to generate forecast of possible driver fluctuations with one single click. Customers on quarterly release will enjoy this benefit from QRC3 onwards. With the integration, users can automatically generate boundaries for driver fluctuations based on analysis of the historical data, without the hassle of technical setup.

One-Click-Trigger to insert predicted ranges for driver fluctuations

 

 

 

How Does it Work?

Smart Boundaries can be triggered by a new button on the drivers list, with two options for predicted results: Either as absolute numbers or as relative percentages. Once triggered, a time series analysis of the available historical data will begin and loading indicators will appear within the input fields for the min/max values, concluding by feeding the results directly into the input fields once ready. You can then proceed to run the scenario with the suggested boundaries, or fine-tune the results manually first, if preferred. A prediction process can be aborted any time before its conclusion by clicking on the same button again.

Choose between absolute or relative values (%) for min/max boundaries

Range of Historical Data Used for Prediction
All historical data from the version used in the scenario will be analysed, up to the cut-off date, which is the configured data filter for the simulation driver. E.g. if the driver filter is “Gross Revenue Q4 2026” and the scenario is based on the version “Actuals”, then all available data for gross revenue in that version up to the last period before Q4 2026 will be taken into consideration. 
Note that the minimum ratio of historical data to result would be around 1:1, but depending on factors such as seasonality or data quality, more data might be required to produce results. Therefore, the recommended amount of historical data would be two to three years to capture cycles and trend.

 

Use Cases & Benefits

Generally, this feature is beneficial in cases where assistance is required for modelling uncertainty, including the following:

Understanding the Corresponding Risk of Underlying Trend: If keen to explore the question of "what are the risks if things continue the way as before", a scenario can be run using Smart Boundaries suggestions for key driver performances without adjustments, to simulate the compound effects for uncertainties across key drivers resulting from the historical development.
For this approach, only Smart Boundaries results are used to model key driver uncertainties.

Pre-configure Risk Guide Rails: Smart Boundaries can also be used to pre-populate the value configuration with guide rails derived from historical data, which can be used as a starting point to model mitigation strategy. This could be also particularly useful in cases where IT is creating a compass simulation to be handed over to business end users for further simulation. Instead of creating a simulation artifact with empty configurations for driver min/max values, IT could already pre-fill the driver list with Smart Boundaries results.
For this approach, end users will utilize Smart Boundaries insights and build on top of those by modifying them.

Accelerated Decision Intelligence: Often, a single user will not have the complete knowledge of possible fluctuation for all key drivers spanning across different regions, departments or line of businesses. Most likely, the end users are either experts in their domains or involved in discussions pertaining to drivers in their responsibilities and could model the uncertainties for those, but not the rest, for which they would need to seek insights from other topic experts or regions/departments. For situations where quick simulations are crucial to judge the current risk exposure and mitigation options, this could mean losing precious time for time-consuming alignments. In such cases Smart Boundaries could assist to fill in the blanks by modelling the uncertainties for drivers where the current trend is to be assumed.
For this approach, end users will be simulating with a mixture of drivers configured by Smart Boundaries and manual configurations.

 

FAQ

Is there an active link between the suggested boundaries and the model data?
No. Once generated, the boundaries are just any other input entry. It neither changes nor updates itself, and can be used as guide rails to be overwritten with new insights, if desired. 

Can I choose which range of data to use for the prediction?
No. Smart Boundaries will take all available data within the version used in the scenario, right up to the last period before the time filter definition for the driver. For example, if the driver is filtered down to Q1 2027, then all prior data in that version up to Q4 2026 will be used for the boundary prediction.

How much historical data would I need?
The minimum ratio is 1:1, so if generating predictions for a driver filtered down to Q1 2027, you will need at least one quarter of historical data. And if it is filtered down to 2027, you will need a year of data. But depending on the variability of your data, sometimes more data might be required to capture the seasonality and trend before results can be produced. Therefore a good rule of thumb is to have at least around two to three years of historical data.  

 

Conclusion

Smart Boundaries brings the best of both worlds together by using time series forecast to model driver uncertainties in compass. Depending on use case and usage, it can reveal corresponding risk for underlying the development trend, serve as guide rail for mitigation simulation, or provide fluctuations inputs for drivers where another source of insight is not at hand.

entry.title: Introducing Support Content — the additional Customer Support knowledge repository
entry.content:
Support Content is a curated knowledge hub that explains SAP systems from the perspective of Customer Support experts. Built from real Customer Support experience, it documents product logic, troubleshooting approaches, and recommended practices — helping you resolve issues faster and understand system behavior.

What you'll find there

Support Content provides practical information about SAP products and scenarios, including:

  • Explanations of system logic and expected behavior
  • Business process guidance and context
  • Step‑by‑step troubleshooting guides
  • Common configuration scenarios
  • Frequently asked questions (FAQs)
  • White papers and architecture examples
  • Best practices and recommendations

Content covers a broad range of SAP offerings — from newer topics such as SAP Analytics Cloud and SAP HANA Smart Data Integration to long‑standing products such as SAP Crystal Reports, SAP Datasphere, and SAP Extended Warehouse Management (EWM) — and everything that falls under Customer Support’s scope.

How it’s used and maintained

Support Content is designed to be a practical reference for customers. Entries are linked to other SAP repositories and are frequently referenced to speed diagnosis and resolution. The repository is reviewed and updated over time, with new topics added according to demand and evolving product landscapes.

How can you share feedback?

Feedback is welcome and helps the repository stay accurate and useful. Use the platform’s comments feature on each page to:

  • Report unclear or out‑of‑date information
  • Suggest additional scenarios or examples
  • Reference related support cases or product versions for context

You have two options to help us to improve the content:

  • Feedback (anonymous): using the Feedback feature on the right-hand side of the page, where you can tell us how helpful the content is and add an optional text.
  • Comment (need an authenticated user): you can provide your comments and be specific on which piece of the text you are referring to.
    First, select the words and click on the button:

    Then, you can add your comment:

* The Comment option will be gradually introduced on the topics. 

When commenting, include a brief description of the issue or the suggested change to help reviewers act more quickly.

Why use Support Content

  • Saves time by providing authoritative explanations and troubleshooting steps
  • Helps you understand root causes and expected system behavior
  • Complements other SAP knowledge sources by focusing on practical, support‑driven guidance

 Explore the Support Content to find product guidance, scenario examples, troubleshooting resources, and much more, tailored to help with your support needs and make the most of your SAP landscape.

Want to know where Support Content originates? Look at this blog post.

Cheers!!
Marcelo Pinheiro
Support Content Business Owner

entry.title: SAP Enterprise Planning: Where AI meets strategy and execution
entry.content:
SAP Sapphire introduced a new vision for Enterprise Planning, and it marks an important step forward for organizations looking to connect strategy with execution through AI.

If you work with SAP Analytics Cloud for Planning or SAP Business Data Cloud, this webinar will help you understand where these solutions are heading and why that matters for your customers. You'll see how AI agents, trusted business data, and managed business content come together to simplify planning, accelerate decision making, and create a more connected planning experience.

The thing is, planning has become much more than creating budgets or forecasts. It now means responding faster to change while keeping finance, operations, and strategy aligned.

Join us to explore SAP's roadmap for Enterprise Planning, discover the innovations announced at SAP Sapphire, and learn how today's SAP SAC and SAP Business Data Cloud investments are shaping the next generation of intelligent planning.

Register now and be among the first to explore what's next. https://partneredge.sap.com/en/library/education/psd/2026/jun/e_oe_te_w_PSD_WEB_00014536.html

#SAP #SAPBTP #SAPPartners #SAPEcosystem #BusinessAI #SAPAnalyticsCloud #BusinessDataCloud #EnterprisePlanning

entry.title: SAP Analytics Cloud Planning Now Available in Support Content Repository
entry.content:
What's New?

We're excited to announce a new dedicated topic SAP Analytics Cloud Planning in our Support Content repository! This addition brings together essential guidance on planning capabilities to help you make the most of your strategic initiatives.

What You'll Find There

When end users encounter issues with SAP Analytics Cloud Planning, for example a cell that won't accept input, a data action that writes nothing, they need guidance and answers that are not covered or beyond the standard Online Help and existing KBAs. That is exactly why we published this Support Content: written to bridge the gap between "something is wrong" and "here is exactly why, and here is what to do about it."

It captures the detailed configuration steps with screenshots, nuanced product behaviors, known limitations, and non-obvious root causes with samples, so that audience can understand, resolve issues faster and gain knowledge from the documentation.

The current SAC Planning Support Content covers the following topics:

  • Data Entry & Publishing - How the two-phase workflow protects data integrity, and what to check when users see inconsistent data between sessions.
  • Planning Area - Why configuring this feature in Model Settings can transform performance on large models, and the edge cases that affect cell inputability.
  • Data Locking & Data Access Control - How two independent gatekeepers interact, and the most common reasons edits are silently blocked.
  • Validation Rules & Member Derivation - Enforcing valid dimension combinations at the model level, including disaggregation behavior and current limits.
  • Data Actions - Queuing mechanics, the CONFIG.HIERARCHY setting that fixes the most common "zero records affected" mystery, and when to use synchronous mode.
  • Audit Log Management - Row limits, cleanup cadence recommendations, and version size impact.
  • Scripting Best Practices - API pitfalls around getMembers(), filter format mismatches, and the scripting patterns that cause render loops or unnecessary backend calls.

Whether you are an implementation consultant, customer administrator, or support engineer, this Support Content provides clear, actionable guidance to turn confusing problems into resolved ones, and it continues to evolve with new and refined topics.

Share Your Feedback

This is a growing resource! So if you have any questions about the content, please go ahead with the embedded feedback or comment feature.

Learn More About Support Content

The Support Content repository is the place to find content created and maintained by SAP Support engineers experties on their areas. Explore this repository with additional documentation provided to help the SAP ecosystem.
